1:
"Thou shalt not sacrifice unto the LORD thy God any bullock, or sheep, wherein is blemish, or any evilfavouredness: for that is an abomination unto the LORD thy God." The witnesses note that no blemished beast was to be sacrificed — an abomination to the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes no sacrifice of any sort was to have a blemish, typical of the unblemished and immaculate Lamb of God, who, without sin, offered himself without spot to God. The witnesses note that a blemished offering was an abomination. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no blemish — nothing maimed or faulty; the nor evilfavouredness — the offering must be perfect; the an abomination — a blemished gift loathed by God; the the unblemished Lamb — christ offered without spot (Heb. 9:14).

The witnesses agree the demand for an unblemished sacrifice both honoured God and pointed to Christ: nothing maimed or faulty might be laid on his altar, for the offering must be perfect, and to bring a blemished beast was "an abomination"; Gill draws the type plainly — the spotless victim figured "the unblemished and immaculate Lamb of God," who being without sin "offered himself without spot to God" (Heb. 9:14); the rule teaches both that God will be served with our best and that only the perfect sacrifice of Christ truly takes away sin.

So the witnesses make the verse no blemished offering: The witnesses note the no blemish: nothing maimed or faulty. The witnesses note the nor evilfavouredness: the offering must be perfect. The witnesses note the an abomination: a blemished gift loathed by God. The witnesses note the the unblemished Lamb: Christ offered without spot (Heb. 9:14). The expositors observe the no blemish: nothing maimed or faulty. The expositors observe the nor evilfavouredness: the offering must be perfect. The expositors observe the an abomination: a blemished gift loathed by God. The expositors observe the the unblemished Lamb: Christ offered without spot (Heb. 9:14). The believer is taught to mark the no blemish, the nor evilfavouredness, the an abomination, the the unblemished Lamb, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that no blemished beast was to be sacrificed, but an abomination — the perfect offering honouring God and pointing to Christ, the immaculate Lamb who offered himself without spot (Heb. 9:14).
2:
"If there be found among you, within any of thy gates... man or woman, that hath wrought wickedness in the sight of the LORD thy God, in transgressing his covenant," The witnesses note the case of an idolater found among them, transgressing God's covenant. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was in any of their cities in the land of Canaan. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the man or woman that hath wrought wickedness in transgressing his covenant. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the found among you — within their gates; the man or woman — all alike under the law; the wrought wickedness — the gravest crime; the transgressing his covenant — idolatry as covenant-treason.

The witnesses agree the verse opens the gravest of crimes — covenant-breaking idolatry: a man or woman found serving other gods had "wrought wickedness" against the LORD and broken the covenant, the most heinous offence in a people bound to him alone; the naming of "man or woman" shows the law fell on all alike without respect of persons, and the framing of idolatry as covenant-treason marks how seriously God regarded any turning from him — not a private fault but a breach of the sacred bond, a treason against the King of Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse covenant-breaking: The witnesses note the found among you: within their gates. The witnesses note the man or woman: all alike under the law. The witnesses note the wrought wickedness: the gravest crime. The witnesses note the transgressing his covenant: idolatry as covenant-treason. The expositors observe the found among you: within their gates. The expositors observe the man or woman: all alike under the law. The expositors observe the wrought wickedness: the gravest crime. The expositors observe the transgressing his covenant: idolatry as covenant-treason. The believer is taught to mark the found among you, the man or woman, the wrought wickedness, the transgressing his covenant, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that an idolater found among them had transgressed God's covenant — idolatry the gravest crime, a treason against the King, the law falling on all alike without respect of persons.
3:
"And hath gone and served other gods, and worshipped them, either the sun, or moon, or any of the host of heaven, which I have not commanded;" The witnesses note the form of the idolatry — worshipping the sun, moon, or host of heaven. The witnesses note that Gill, with the Targum, notes the going after the evil imagination, lusting after other lovers, forsaking the true God and his worship. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the serving of the sun, moon, or host of heaven, which God had not commanded. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the served other gods — forsaking the true God; the the sun, moon, host of heaven — the bright creation adored; the which I have not commanded — forbidden worship; the the creature above the Creator — the works made a rival to God (Rom. 1:25).

The witnesses agree the verse names the worship of the heavenly bodies as a chief form of idolatry: to serve the sun, moon, or host of heaven was to give to the creature the honour due the Creator, worshipping "which I have not commanded"; the folly and sin of it is that it adores the works of God's hands instead of God himself, the bright creation made a rival to its Maker; the law brands this as forbidden worship, teaching that whatever is set in God's place — however glorious in itself — is an idol, and that the creature is never to be served above the Creator (cf. Rom. 1:25).

So the witnesses make the verse worshipping the host: The witnesses note the served other gods: forsaking the true God. The witnesses note the the sun, moon, host of heaven: the bright creation adored. The witnesses note the which I have not commanded: forbidden worship. The witnesses note the the creature above the Creator: the works made a rival to God (Rom. 1:25). The expositors observe the served other gods: forsaking the true God. The expositors observe the the sun, moon, host of heaven: the bright creation adored. The expositors observe the which I have not commanded: forbidden worship. The expositors observe the the creature above the Creator: the works made a rival to God (Rom. 1:25). The believer is taught to mark the served other gods, the the sun, moon, host of heaven, the which I have not commanded, the the creature above the Creator, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that they worshipped the sun, moon, or host of heaven — adoring the creature above the Creator, the bright works of God made a rival to their Maker, forbidden worship (Rom. 1:25).
4:
"And it be told thee, and thou hast heard of it, and enquired diligently, and, behold, it be true, and the thing certain, that such abomination is wrought in Israel;" The witnesses note the requirement of diligent inquiry before any judgment — the report tested, the truth made certain. The witnesses note that Gill notes a report of this kind was not to be neglected, yet not concluded on by hearsay; it was to be looked into and the witnesses thoroughly examined. The witnesses note that the matter must be found true and certain. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the it be told thee — the report received; the enquired diligently — the witnesses examined; the true and certain — the fact established; the justice on proof — none condemned on rumour (Prov. 18:13).

The witnesses agree the law guards justice with a careful inquiry: even so grave a charge as idolatry was not to be acted on by rumour, but "enquired diligently," the witnesses examined and the thing proved "true and certain" before any sentence; the rule shows God's care that no one suffer on mere report, and that justice rest on established fact — a standing pattern that grave accusations are to be sifted to the truth before judgment, neither the crime ignored nor the accused condemned unheard (cf. Prov. 18:13).

So the witnesses make the verse enquire diligently: The witnesses note the it be told thee: the report received. The witnesses note the enquired diligently: the witnesses examined. The witnesses note the true and certain: the fact established. The witnesses note the justice on proof: none condemned on rumour (Prov. 18:13). The expositors observe the it be told thee: the report received. The expositors observe the enquired diligently: the witnesses examined. The expositors observe the true and certain: the fact established. The expositors observe the justice on proof: none condemned on rumour (Prov. 18:13). The believer is taught to mark the it be told thee, the enquired diligently, the true and certain, the justice on proof, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that the charge was to be enquired into diligently and proved true and certain — justice resting on established fact, none condemned on mere report, the grave accusation sifted to the truth (Prov. 18:13).
5:
"Then shalt thou bring forth that man or that woman... and shalt stone them with stones, till they die." The witnesses note the sentence — the convicted idolater brought forth and stoned to death. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was after being brought before a court, tried, found guilty, and sentenced, then brought forth to execution. The witnesses note that they were to be stoned with stones till dead. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the bring forth that man — after lawful trial; the that man or woman — the convicted idolater; the stone them with stones — the public execution; the the evil put away — the nation's holiness guarded (1 Cor. 5:13).

The witnesses agree the execution followed only upon lawful trial and conviction: the idolater was not slain in haste but "brought forth" after due process — examined, found guilty, and sentenced — then publicly stoned; the orderly procedure shows that even the gravest punishment was bound to law, not passion, and the public stoning marked the people's united repudiation of idolatry; the severity, terrible to flesh, was God's judicial sentence on the covenant-traitor, the holiness of the nation requiring that the evil be put away (cf. 1 Cor. 5:13, put away from among yourselves that wicked person).

So the witnesses make the verse the sentence carried: The witnesses note the bring forth that man: after lawful trial. The witnesses note the that man or woman: the convicted idolater. The witnesses note the stone them with stones: the public execution. The witnesses note the the evil put away: the nation's holiness guarded (1 Cor. 5:13). The expositors observe the bring forth that man: after lawful trial. The expositors observe the that man or woman: the convicted idolater. The expositors observe the stone them with stones: the public execution. The expositors observe the the evil put away: the nation's holiness guarded (1 Cor. 5:13). The believer is taught to mark the bring forth that man, the that man or woman, the stone them with stones, the the evil put away, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that the convicted idolater was brought forth and stoned — the punishment bound to lawful trial, the public sentence the nation's repudiation of idolatry, the evil put away (1 Cor. 5:13).
6:
"At the mouth of two witnesses, or three witnesses, shall he that is worthy of death be put to death; but at the mouth of one witness he shall not be put to death." The witnesses note that a capital sentence required two or three witnesses — never one alone. The witnesses note that Gill notes the idolater was stoned; two witnesses were sufficient, three the better, but on the testimony of one, sentence might not be pronounced. The witnesses note that one witness could not condemn to death. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the two or three witnesses — required for death; the worthy of death — the guilty idolater; the not at one witness — never on a single word; the truth doubly sure — every matter established (Matt. 18:16).

The witnesses agree the requirement of two or three witnesses guarded life against false or single testimony: no man, however guilty, was to die "at the mouth of one witness," but only on the agreeing word of two or three; the rule shows God's tender justice, that the truth be doubly sure before blood is shed, protecting the accused from malice or mistake; the principle runs into the New Testament for the establishing of every matter (Matt. 18:16; 2 Cor. 13:1), that truth be confirmed by sufficient witness before any are condemned.

So the witnesses make the verse two or three witnesses: The witnesses note the two or three witnesses: required for death. The witnesses note the worthy of death: the guilty idolater. The witnesses note the not at one witness: never on a single word. The witnesses note the truth doubly sure: every matter established (Matt. 18:16). The expositors observe the two or three witnesses: required for death. The expositors observe the worthy of death: the guilty idolater. The expositors observe the not at one witness: never on a single word. The expositors observe the truth doubly sure: every matter established (Matt. 18:16). The believer is taught to mark the two or three witnesses, the worthy of death, the not at one witness, the truth doubly sure, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that a capital sentence required two or three witnesses, never one — God's tender justice guarding life, the truth doubly sure before blood is shed, every matter established by sufficient witness (Matt. 18:16).
7:
"The hands of the witnesses shall be first upon him to put him to death, and afterward the hands of all the people..." The witnesses note that the witnesses themselves were to cast the first stones, then all the people. The witnesses note that Gill, with Aben Ezra, notes they were to cast the first stone, which was a further trial of their testimony; their readiness without reluctance showed their zeal for God's honour and the truth of their witness. The witnesses note that afterward all the people joined. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the witnesses' hands first — casting the first stones; the a test of their witness — the false might shrink; the then all the people — the community sharing; the judgment's weight borne — witnesses pressed to truth, all to horror of sin.

The witnesses agree the witnesses' casting of the first stone was both a test and a solemn warning: by requiring them to begin the execution, the law tried the sincerity of their testimony — a false witness might well shrink from shedding the blood he had sworn against — and laid on them the weight of the death they caused; the people then joined, that the whole community share in putting away the evil; the solemn rite pressed witnesses to truthfulness and all to a sober horror of the sin, the burden of judgment borne by those who testified and the nation alike.

So the witnesses make the verse the witnesses first: The witnesses note the the witnesses' hands first: casting the first stones. The witnesses note the a test of their witness: the false might shrink. The witnesses note the then all the people: the community sharing. The witnesses note the judgment's weight borne: witnesses pressed to truth, all to horror of sin. The expositors observe the the witnesses' hands first: casting the first stones. The expositors observe the a test of their witness: the false might shrink. The expositors observe the then all the people: the community sharing. The expositors observe the judgment's weight borne: witnesses pressed to truth, all to horror of sin. The believer is taught to mark the the witnesses' hands first, the a test of their witness, the then all the people, the judgment's weight borne, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that the witnesses were to cast the first stones, then all the people — the rite testing their sincerity and laying on them the weight of the death, the whole community sharing in putting away the evil.
8:
"If there arise a matter too hard for thee in judgment... being matters of controversy within thy gates: then shalt thou arise, and get thee up into the place which the LORD thy God shall choose;" The witnesses note the provision for hard cases — too difficult for local judges, to be carried to the chosen place. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is spoken to inferior judges in the country cities, who might have cases too wonderful, mysterious, or hidden, beyond their capacity to determine. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set such matters carried up to the place the LORD should choose. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a matter too hard — beyond the local judges; the matters of controversy — difficult and disputed; the get thee up — to the chosen place; the justice in degrees — the harder referred to greater wisdom.

The witnesses agree the law provided a higher court for cases beyond the local judges: when a matter was "too hard," controverted and difficult, the inferior judges were to bring it to the chosen place, to the supreme tribunal there; the arrangement shows God's wise ordering of justice in degrees, that no case be left unresolved nor decided wrongly through ignorance, the harder matters referred to greater wisdom; it teaches the humility that knows the bounds of its own judgment and seeks higher counsel, and the order of a settled system of justice under God.

So the witnesses make the verse hard cases: The witnesses note the a matter too hard: beyond the local judges. The witnesses note the matters of controversy: difficult and disputed. The witnesses note the get thee up: to the chosen place. The witnesses note the justice in degrees: the harder referred to greater wisdom. The expositors observe the a matter too hard: beyond the local judges. The expositors observe the matters of controversy: difficult and disputed. The expositors observe the get thee up: to the chosen place. The expositors observe the justice in degrees: the harder referred to greater wisdom. The believer is taught to mark the a matter too hard, the matters of controversy, the get thee up, the justice in degrees, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that hard cases were to be carried to the chosen place — God's wise ordering of justice in degrees, the difficult referred to greater wisdom, no case left unresolved or wrongly judged.
9:
"And thou shalt come unto the priests the Levites, and unto the judge that shall be in those days, and enquire; and they shall shew thee the sentence of judgment:" The witnesses note the supreme tribunal — the priests and the judge — to whom the hard case was brought. The witnesses note that Gill notes the priests of the tribe of Levi, and that Maimonides observes the great sanhedrin should have priests and Levites in it. The witnesses note that they should show the sentence of judgment. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the priests the Levites — the spiritual authority; the the judge — the civil authority; the enquire of them — the supreme tribunal; the justice on God's law — decided by those learned in his statutes.

The witnesses agree the highest court joined the spiritual and the civil: the hard case was brought to "the priests the Levites" and "the judge," the sanhedrin in which both priestly and judicial wisdom met, that the sentence be given with the fullest knowledge of God's law; the union of priest and judge in the supreme tribunal shows that true justice rests on the law of God, the cause decided not by mere human prudence but by those learned in his statutes — the highest appeal being to the mind of God as taught by his appointed ministers.

So the witnesses make the verse priests and judge: The witnesses note the the priests the Levites: the spiritual authority. The witnesses note the the judge: the civil authority. The witnesses note the enquire of them: the supreme tribunal. The witnesses note the justice on God's law: decided by those learned in his statutes. The expositors observe the the priests the Levites: the spiritual authority. The expositors observe the the judge: the civil authority. The expositors observe the enquire of them: the supreme tribunal. The expositors observe the justice on God's law: decided by those learned in his statutes. The believer is taught to mark the the priests the Levites, the the judge, the enquire of them, the justice on God's law, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that the hard case was brought to the priests and the judge — the supreme tribunal joining spiritual and civil wisdom, true justice resting on the law of God taught by his ministers.
10:
"And thou shalt do according to the sentence, which they of that place which the LORD shall choose shall shew thee; and thou shalt observe to do according to all that they inform thee:" The witnesses note the duty of the lower judges to abide by the sentence of the supreme court. The witnesses note that Gill notes the inferior courts were to return and proceed according to the judgment of the great court at Jerusalem. The witnesses note that Wesley sets the lower judge bound to follow the higher court's direction. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the do according to the sentence — abide by the higher court; the which they shall shew — the supreme decision; the observe to do all — submission to authority; the order in justice — the lower deferring to the higher (Heb. 13:17).

The witnesses agree the verse enjoins submission to the higher authority: the local judges, having brought the hard case up, were to "do according to the sentence" of the supreme court, abiding by its decision; the rule maintains the order and unity of justice, that the lower defer to the higher and the law be applied with one mind throughout the land; it teaches the necessary subordination within any rightly-ordered society and church, that men submit to lawful authority and not every one do what is right in his own eyes (cf. Heb. 13:17).

So the witnesses make the verse do according to the sentence: The witnesses note the do according to the sentence: abide by the higher court. The witnesses note the which they shall shew: the supreme decision. The witnesses note the observe to do all: submission to authority. The witnesses note the order in justice: the lower deferring to the higher (Heb. 13:17). The expositors observe the do according to the sentence: abide by the higher court. The expositors observe the which they shall shew: the supreme decision. The expositors observe the observe to do all: submission to authority. The expositors observe the order in justice: the lower deferring to the higher (Heb. 13:17). The believer is taught to mark the do according to the sentence, the which they shall shew, the observe to do all, the order in justice, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that the lower judges were to do according to the sentence of the supreme court — the order and unity of justice maintained, the necessary submission to lawful authority within a rightly-ordered society (Heb. 13:17).
11:
"According to the sentence of the law which they shall teach thee... thou shalt not decline from the sentence which they shall shew thee, to the right hand, nor to the left." The witnesses note that the supreme court taught the law, not new laws, and from their sentence none was to decline. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were not to make any new law, but to teach the law of God; so far as their judgment agreed with that law they were to be regarded. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the duty not to decline from their sentence. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the sentence of the law — they taught, not made law; the which they shall teach — declaring God's statutes; the decline not — neither right nor left; the authority under the word — the ruling weighty as God's law (Mal. 2:7).

The witnesses agree the authority of the court was bounded by the law of God: they were "not to make any new law, but to teach the law of God," and their sentence was binding "so far as" it agreed with that law; the submission required was therefore not blind, but a deference to those who declared God's own statutes — the people not to swerve from a judgment grounded in the law, "to the right hand, nor to the left"; the rule honours both lawful authority and the supremacy of God's word, the judge's ruling weighty because it teaches the divine law (cf. Mal. 2:7).

So the witnesses make the verse the sentence of the law: The witnesses note the the sentence of the law: they taught, not made law. The witnesses note the which they shall teach: declaring God's statutes. The witnesses note the decline not: neither right nor left. The witnesses note the authority under the word: the ruling weighty as God's law (Mal. 2:7). The expositors observe the the sentence of the law: they taught, not made law. The expositors observe the which they shall teach: declaring God's statutes. The expositors observe the decline not: neither right nor left. The expositors observe the authority under the word: the ruling weighty as God's law (Mal. 2:7). The believer is taught to mark the the sentence of the law, the which they shall teach, the decline not, the authority under the word, and that even kings are under the law of God. The verse thus records that none was to decline from the court's sentence, which taught God's law — authority bounded by the word, the judge's ruling weighty because it declared the divine statutes (Mal. 2:7).
12:
"And the man that will do presumptuously, and will not hearken unto the priest... or unto the judge, even that man shall die: and thou shalt put away the evil from Israel." The witnesses note the presumptuous man who defies the court's authority put to death, that evil be put away. The witnesses note that Gill notes the country judge who, after applying to Jerusalem, is conceited in his own opinion, rejects theirs, and obstinately takes his own way. The witnesses note that Clarke sets the man who acts presumptuously. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the do presumptuously — defying the court; the not hearken — rejecting the sentence; the that man shall die — the grave offence; the put away the evil — order guarded from contempt (Rom. 13:2).

The witnesses agree the contempt of lawful authority was treated as a grave and capital offence: the man who "presumptuously" refused the sentence of the supreme court, clinging obstinately to his own opinion, was to die, that such defiance not spread; the severity marks how seriously God regarded the overturning of order and the contempt of his appointed authority, the proud self-will that sets itself above lawful judgment being a poison to the commonwealth; the law thus guards the peace and order of the nation by punishing the rebellious contempt that would dissolve it (cf. Rom. 13:2).

So the witnesses make the verse the presumptuous man: The witnesses note the do presumptuously: defying the court. The witnesses note the not hearken: rejecting the sentence. The witnesses note the that man shall die: the grave offence. The witnesses note the put away the evil: order guarded from contempt (Rom. 13:2). The expositors observe the do presumptuously: defying the court. The expositors observe the not hearken: rejecting the sentence. The expositors observe the that man shall die: the grave offence. The expositors observe the put away the evil: order guarded from contempt (Rom. 13:2). The believer is taught to mark the do presumptuously, the not hearken, the that man shall die, the put away the evil, and that even kings are under the law of God. The verse thus records that the presumptuous man who defied the court was to die, that evil be put away — God guarding order and the peace of the nation against the proud self-will that would dissolve it (Rom. 13:2).
13:
"And all the people shall hear, and fear, and do no more presumptuously." The witnesses note the deterrent purpose — all hearing of the judgment would fear and not offend likewise. The witnesses note that Gill notes all the people, and particularly the judges in their cities, would hear what was done to the obstinate elder and be afraid to commit the like. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the deterrent — all the people shall hear and fear. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the people shall hear — of the judgment; the and fear — the wholesome dread; the do no more presumptuously — restrained from the like; the judgment a mercy to all — one punished that others fear (1 Tim. 5:20).

The witnesses agree the public punishment served a teaching end — the fear that restrains: when all heard of the doom of the presumptuous, they would "fear, and do no more presumptuously," the example deterring others from the like contempt; the law thus uses the just punishment of one to instruct and warn the many, the wholesome fear of consequence guarding the community from rebellion; it shows that judgment rightly executed is not cruelty but a mercy to the whole, restraining sin by the salutary dread of its penalty (cf. 1 Tim. 5:20, that others also may fear).

So the witnesses make the verse hear and fear: The witnesses note the all the people shall hear: of the judgment. The witnesses note the and fear: the wholesome dread. The witnesses note the do no more presumptuously: restrained from the like. The witnesses note the judgment a mercy to all: one punished that others fear (1 Tim. 5:20). The expositors observe the all the people shall hear: of the judgment. The expositors observe the and fear: the wholesome dread. The expositors observe the do no more presumptuously: restrained from the like. The expositors observe the judgment a mercy to all: one punished that others fear (1 Tim. 5:20). The believer is taught to mark the all the people shall hear, the and fear, the do no more presumptuously, the judgment a mercy to all, and that even kings are under the law of God. The verse thus records that all would hear and fear and offend no more — the just punishment of one a warning to the many, the salutary dread of penalty restraining the community from rebellion (1 Tim. 5:20).
14:
"When thou art come unto the land... and shalt say, I will set a king over me, like as all the nations that are about me;" The witnesses note the foreseen desire for a king, like the nations — the occasion of the law of the king. The witnesses note that Gill notes this concerns coming into the land of Canaan. The witnesses note that Henry notes that after the laws for subjects fitly follow laws concerning kings, for those that rule others must remember they are themselves under command. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the come unto the land — the future settlement; the I will set a king — the foreseen desire; the like the nations — the lower motive; the rulers under command — the king himself bound by law.

The puritan expositor (Henry) marks the fitting order and the great principle: after the laws for subjects come the laws for kings, "for those that rule others must themselves remember that they are under command"; God foresaw Israel would one day desire a king "like all the nations," and provided beforehand for it — the very framing of the law a quiet caution, that the wish to be like the nations was not the highest motive, yet God in mercy regulated what he foresaw, binding even the future king under his own law, that no ruler be above the King of kings.

So the witnesses make the verse a king like the nations: The witnesses note the come unto the land: the future settlement. The witnesses note the I will set a king: the foreseen desire. The witnesses note the like the nations: the lower motive. The witnesses note the rulers under command: the king himself bound by law. The expositors observe the come unto the land: the future settlement. The expositors observe the I will set a king: the foreseen desire. The expositors observe the like the nations: the lower motive. The expositors observe the rulers under command: the king himself bound by law. The believer is taught to mark the come unto the land, the I will set a king, the like the nations, the rulers under command, and that even kings are under the law of God. The verse thus records that God foresaw Israel would desire a king like the nations — providing beforehand, binding even the future king under his law, that those who rule others remember they are themselves under command.
15:
"Thou shalt in any wise set him king over thee, whom the LORD thy God shall choose... thou mayest not set a stranger over thee, which is not thy brother." The witnesses note that the king must be of God's choosing and from among the brethren, not a stranger. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was not a command to set a king but a permission, in case they desired one, with the restriction to appoint none but whom God should choose. The witnesses note that he was to be from among their brethren, not a stranger. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the whom the LORD shall choose — the throne under God; the set him king — his vicegerent, not the people's mere choice; the from among thy brethren — bound to his people; the not a stranger — the King made our brother (Heb. 2:17).

The witnesses agree the law set two great bounds upon the kingship: the king must be "whom the LORD thy God shall choose" and one "from among thy brethren," not a foreigner; the first kept the throne under God's appointment, the king his vicegerent and not the people's mere choice; the second kept the ruler bound to his people by kinship and the covenant, that he share their faith and not bring in the ways of the stranger; both point to the true King, Christ, chosen of God and made our brother, "in all things made like unto his brethren" (Heb. 2:17), the King after God's own heart and of our own nature.

So the witnesses make the verse the king God chooses: The witnesses note the whom the LORD shall choose: the throne under God. The witnesses note the set him king: his vicegerent, not the people's mere choice. The witnesses note the from among thy brethren: bound to his people. The witnesses note the not a stranger: the King made our brother (Heb. 2:17). The expositors observe the whom the LORD shall choose: the throne under God. The expositors observe the set him king: his vicegerent, not the people's mere choice. The expositors observe the from among thy brethren: bound to his people. The expositors observe the not a stranger: the King made our brother (Heb. 2:17). The believer is taught to mark the whom the LORD shall choose, the set him king, the from among thy brethren, the not a stranger, and that even kings are under the law of God. The verse thus records that the king must be God's choice and from among the brethren — the throne under God's appointment, the ruler bound to his people, pointing to Christ the King chosen of God and made our brother (Heb. 2:17).
16:
"But he shall not multiply horses to himself, nor cause the people to return to Egypt, to the end that he should multiply horses..." The witnesses note the first restraint on the king — not to multiply horses nor turn the people back to Egypt. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was that he might not put his trust in outward things, as some trust in horses and chariots, nor oppress his subjects with a standing army. The witnesses note that he was to keep no more horses than for his own use. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not multiply horses — no amassed cavalry; the return not to Egypt — no retracing the path of bondage; the not trust in might — leaning on God, not flesh; the safety in God — not in chariots (Ps. 20:7).

The witnesses agree the ban on multiplying horses guarded both faith and freedom: the king was not to amass cavalry — the strength of the nations — lest he "trust in horses and chariots" rather than the LORD, nor turn the people back toward Egypt for them, retracing the path of bondage; the law would have the king lean on God for his defence, not on military might, and keep his people from the ways of the world they were redeemed from; it teaches that true safety is in God, not in the arm of flesh, "some trust in chariots... but we will remember the name of the LORD" (Ps. 20:7).

So the witnesses make the verse not multiply horses: The witnesses note the not multiply horses: no amassed cavalry. The witnesses note the return not to Egypt: no retracing the path of bondage. The witnesses note the not trust in might: leaning on God, not flesh. The witnesses note the safety in God: not in chariots (Ps. 20:7). The expositors observe the not multiply horses: no amassed cavalry. The expositors observe the return not to Egypt: no retracing the path of bondage. The expositors observe the not trust in might: leaning on God, not flesh. The expositors observe the safety in God: not in chariots (Ps. 20:7). The believer is taught to mark the not multiply horses, the return not to Egypt, the not trust in might, the safety in God, and that even kings are under the law of God. The verse thus records that the king was not to multiply horses nor return to Egypt — leaning on God for defence, not military might, true safety in the LORD and not in the arm of flesh (Ps. 20:7).
17:
"Neither shall he multiply wives to himself, that his heart turn not away: neither shall he greatly multiply to himself silver and gold." The witnesses note the further restraints — not to multiply wives, lest his heart turn away, nor to heap up silver and gold. The witnesses note that Gill notes the multiplying of wives would turn his heart from the duty of his office and from religion, as Solomon's heart was turned by his idolatrous wives. The witnesses note that he was not greatly to multiply silver and gold. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not multiply wives — lest his heart turn away; the that his heart turn not — the snare of lust; the not multiply silver and gold — the snare of greed; the the heart guarded — drawn from God by indulgence (1 Kings 11:4).

The witnesses agree these bans guarded the king's heart from the two great snares of rulers — lust and greed: many wives would "turn his heart away," as they did Solomon's, into idolatry and neglect of his office, and heaped-up riches would corrupt him with luxury and pride; the law would keep the king's affections free for God and his people, not enslaved to pleasure or wealth; the warning, written long before Solomon, stands as a prophecy of his fall and a lesson for all in power, that the heart is drawn from God by indulgence, and the ruler must guard it above all (cf. 1 Kings 11:4).

So the witnesses make the verse not multiply wives or gold: The witnesses note the not multiply wives: lest his heart turn away. The witnesses note the that his heart turn not: the snare of lust. The witnesses note the not multiply silver and gold: the snare of greed. The witnesses note the the heart guarded: drawn from God by indulgence (1 Kings 11:4). The expositors observe the not multiply wives: lest his heart turn away. The expositors observe the that his heart turn not: the snare of lust. The expositors observe the not multiply silver and gold: the snare of greed. The expositors observe the the heart guarded: drawn from God by indulgence (1 Kings 11:4). The believer is taught to mark the not multiply wives, the that his heart turn not, the not multiply silver and gold, the the heart guarded, and that even kings are under the law of God. The verse thus records that the king was not to multiply wives or gold, lest his heart turn away — guarding against the snares of lust and greed, a warning fulfilled in Solomon's fall, the heart drawn from God by indulgence (1 Kings 11:4).
18:
"And it shall be, when he sitteth upon the throne of his kingdom, that he shall write him a copy of this law in a book out of that which is before the priests the Levites:" The witnesses note the king commanded to write himself a copy of the law upon taking the throne. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Septuagint and Vulgate interpret "this law" of the book of Deuteronomy, a summary of God's laws to the people. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the king writing a copy from that before the priests. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the upon his throne — his first duty as king; the write him a copy — of God's law; the from the priests — the law in their keeping; the the king under the law — no lawgiver above it.

The witnesses agree the king's first duty on his throne was to put himself under the law of God: he was to "write him a copy of this law," taking it from the keeping of the priests, that he might have God's word ever by him; the requirement marks the great principle of godly rule — that the king is no lawgiver above the law but a subject under it, bound to the same statutes as his people; the writing of it with his own hand was to fix it in his heart, that he govern by the word of God and not his own will, the ruler himself ruled by the King of kings.

So the witnesses make the verse write a copy: The witnesses note the upon his throne: his first duty as king. The witnesses note the write him a copy: of God's law. The witnesses note the from the priests: the law in their keeping. The witnesses note the the king under the law: no lawgiver above it. The expositors observe the upon his throne: his first duty as king. The expositors observe the write him a copy: of God's law. The expositors observe the from the priests: the law in their keeping. The expositors observe the the king under the law: no lawgiver above it. The believer is taught to mark the upon his throne, the write him a copy, the from the priests, the the king under the law, and that even kings are under the law of God. The verse thus records that the king was to write himself a copy of the law — putting himself under God's word, the ruler no lawgiver above the law but a subject under it, himself ruled by the King of kings.
19:
"And it shall be with him, and he shall read therein all the days of his life: that he may learn to fear the LORD his God, to keep all the words of this law..." The witnesses note the king to keep the law by him and read it all his days, that he learn to fear God and obey. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was to be with him always, and he was to read it every day, meditating on it night and day as a good man does. The witnesses note that the end was to learn the fear of the LORD and to keep all the words of the law. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the it shall be with him — the law ever by him; the read therein — all the days of his life; the learn to fear the LORD — the end of his study; the to keep the law — meditating day and night (Ps. 1:2).

The witnesses agree the king was to be a daily student of God's word: the law ever "with him," read "all the days of his life," that by constant meditation he learn to fear God and keep his commandments; the requirement makes the godly ruler first a godly man, his throne founded on his knowledge of and obedience to God's word; the meditation enjoined on the king is the very mark of the blessed man, who "in his law doth he meditate day and night" (Ps. 1:2) — the ruler's wisdom and his people's welfare resting on his reverent study of the law of God.

So the witnesses make the verse read it all his days: The witnesses note the it shall be with him: the law ever by him. The witnesses note the read therein: all the days of his life. The witnesses note the learn to fear the LORD: the end of his study. The witnesses note the to keep the law: meditating day and night (Ps. 1:2). The expositors observe the it shall be with him: the law ever by him. The expositors observe the read therein: all the days of his life. The expositors observe the learn to fear the LORD: the end of his study. The expositors observe the to keep the law: meditating day and night (Ps. 1:2). The believer is taught to mark the it shall be with him, the read therein, the learn to fear the LORD, the to keep the law, and that even kings are under the law of God. The verse thus records that the king was to read the law all his days, that he learn to fear God and keep it — the godly ruler first a godly man, his throne founded on reverent meditation on the word (Ps. 1:2).
20:
"That his heart be not lifted up above his brethren, and that he turn not aside from the commandment... that he may prolong his days in his kingdom, he, and his children..." The witnesses note the end of all these laws — that the king be humble, obedient, and so long established. The witnesses note that Gill notes that his heart be not lifted up above his brethren on account of his office, considering he was subject to the law of God and accountable to him. The witnesses note that he turn not aside from the commandment, that his days be prolonged. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the heart not lifted up — humble above his brethren; the turn not aside — obedient to the commandment; the prolong his days — the throne established by righteousness; the the humble King — christ exalted for his humility (Phil. 2:8-9).

The witnesses agree the great aim of the king's law was humility and obedience, with long establishment as their fruit: knowing himself "subject to the law of God and accountable" to him, the king was not to be "lifted up above his brethren" nor swerve from the commandment, but rule as a brother under God; the promise of a prolonged reign for him and his children shows that the throne is established by righteousness and humility, not by pride or power — a lesson crowned in Christ, the King who, though Lord of all, humbled himself and is exalted forever (cf. Phil. 2:8-9), the humble King whose kingdom has no end.

So the witnesses make the verse not lifted up: The witnesses note the heart not lifted up: humble above his brethren. The witnesses note the turn not aside: obedient to the commandment. The witnesses note the prolong his days: the throne established by righteousness. The witnesses note the the humble King: Christ exalted for his humility (Phil. 2:8-9). The expositors observe the heart not lifted up: humble above his brethren. The expositors observe the turn not aside: obedient to the commandment. The expositors observe the prolong his days: the throne established by righteousness. The expositors observe the the humble King: Christ exalted for his humility (Phil. 2:8-9). The believer is taught to mark the heart not lifted up, the turn not aside, the prolong his days, the the humble King, and that even kings are under the law of God. The verse thus records that the king was to be humble and obedient, that his reign be long — the throne established by righteousness and humility, crowned in Christ the humble King exalted forever (Phil. 2:8-9).
